discussion_posts.name: pilot_n_programmer
discussion_posts.content:
Got this problem at FB yesterday.
I solved it sometime ago on leetcode but during the interview I failed to do it.
It was a second problem to solve, 20 mins left and all this time pressure+stress made me to panic.
I would expect that two problems in 45 mins would be Easy and Medium or worst case Medium/Medium but got Medium and Hard. 
Moral of the story: don't expect that you will get the same complexity tasks like others usually get - I find it is sort of lottery. Prepare for worst to make it done! And don't stress or panic - it doesn't help. Good luck everyone!

discussion_posts.name: matcovic
discussion_posts.content: It really feels bad when you come up with a solution spending hours, only to receive a TLE on the last test cases with a 10^5 input size!

discussion_posts.name: Pratham_Upadhyay
discussion_posts.content: It feels amazing when you can solve a hard question all by yourself! It brings great happiness, right? If you managed to do it on your own, give yourself a thumbs-up! \uD83C\uDF1F

discussion_posts.name: bhanujggandhi
discussion_posts.content: Do not maintain the string variable and update it every time you find a better minimum substring. **Instead maintain pointers and create substring only once, otherwise there will be TLE/MLE**

discussion_posts.name: petia
discussion_posts.content: I don\'t know if it is just me, but I am really tired of the poor explanations of LC problems. Quite often we do not have any input restrictions and bounds. In this problem we don\'t know if we expect duplicate characters in T, if we are looking for each distinct character of T in S, or literally every single one (i.e. T="aa" and S="a" - is this valid S or not...). Every time I need to submit my code to get to yet another failing test because there is some unmentioned restriction or requirement. In a real interview if this is the case I would ask clarifying questions, obviously this is not an option in this case. So annoying!

discussion_posts.name: codewhisperer__
discussion_posts.content: \uD83D\uDE02\uD83D\uDE02\uD83D\uDE02 imagine getting this question in a live interview having never heard it before and answering it correctly on the spot in less than 45 minutes \uD83D\uDE02\uD83D\uDE02\uD83D\uDE02\uD83D\uDE02\uD83D\uDE02\uD83D\uDE02

discussion_posts.name: MyPing0
discussion_posts.content: If you are stuck on the really long test cases, like #266, make sure you are using either .equals() or .intValue() when trying to compare the values in your HashMap\'s\n\nhttps://stackoverflow.com/questions/4428774/why-java-does-not-see-that-integers-are-equal

discussion_posts.name: RyanYibingJia
discussion_posts.content: Just be careful for object `Integer`, using `Integer.intValue()` to compare the value of it.\n\n

discussion_posts.name: ayushabg
discussion_posts.content: Very Badly written Description! I solved the problem believing that we need to match exact frequencies of characters in t in substrings of s. Only to find test case 192 failing. \n"aaaaaaaaaaaabbbbbcdd", "abcdd"  -> "abbbbbcdd"
